items.creator: Insecurity Insight
items.title: Haiti Violence Against Health Care in Conflict 2025
items.author: Insecurity Insight
items.content:
Country: Haiti
Source: Insecurity Insight

Please refer to the attached file.

Key findings:

  • Health worker kidnappings surged and health facilities continued to be attacked. Reports of targeted health worker killings persisted, while patients in hospitals were also targeted.
  • Armed groups opened fire on health facilities and aid convoys, erected barricades, vandalized infrastructure, stole medical equipment and ambulances, and in some cases forced hospitals to suspend their services or evacuate staff.
  • Insecurity disrupted the functioning of health facilities, particularly in the Port-au-Prince metropolitan area, where between 60% and 80% of health facilities were reported to be closed or non-functional because of violence, looting, supply disruptions, and the departure of medical staff.
  • Cuts to USAID funding forced the suspension of key health programs like HIV prevention services and jeopardized years of progress in local clinical care, leaving many communities with reduced access to routine treatment and preventive care.

items.creator: Global Polio Eradication Initiative
items.title: World: Global Polio Eradication Initiative High-Level Interview: Chair of the GPEI Polio Oversight Board & Chair of Rotary’s International PolioPlus Committee Mike McGovern
items.author: Global Polio Eradication Initiative
items.content:
Country: World
Source: Global Polio Eradication Initiative

GPEI Q1. The Global Polio Eradication Initiative (GPEI) is a unique public private partnership with six core partners, including Rotary International. What role do partnerships play in the progress we have made against polio?

When Rotary first dreamed of a polio-free world, we knew we couldn’t make that dream a reality on our own. We are a proud founding partner of the Global Polio Eradication Initiative, a coalition which unites the six core partners, health workers, governments, donors, and global leaders behind the vision of a world where children are forever safe from the threat of polio.

Working in collaboration with governments allows for thousands of polio vaccinators, health workers, and community mobilizers to reach up to 400 million children every year in up to 40 countries with over 1 billion doses of polio vaccines. Since 1988, the GPEI has immunized over 3 billion children, reduced polio cases by 99.9%, eradicated two types of wild poliovirus, and 20 million people are walking today who would have otherwise been paralyzed by polio. That is the impact of partnerships in improving public health.

GPEI Q2. Rotary has a very specific role in the Global Polio Eradication Initiative including fundraising, advocacy and raising awareness. Could you describe some of the activities that Rotary members undertake and explain how they contribute to overall polio eradication efforts?

Rotary members have been, and continue to be, at the very heart of the fight against polio. Each year, they raise US$50 million (tripled to US$150 million thanks to a matching gift from the Gates Foundation) to support polio eradication efforts in endemic and outbreak areas.

There are no limits to the creativity of Rotary members in their support for ending polio.

One of my favorite fundraisers in recent years was the “Flight to End Polio” by Rotarians Peter Teahen, Ed Galkin, and John Ockenfels. In 2023, Teahen and Ockenfels took a literal trip around the world in a single-engine Cessna, visiting 19 countries in 90 days. In 2025, Teahen was joined by Galkin for a similar trip across the Atlantic. Together, both trips raised over US$2 million for polio eradication, and media interest in the story brought greater public awareness to the fight to end polio.

On the advocacy front, many Rotary members host parliamentary events to educate government leaders about polio eradication efforts and to secure political and financial support. And for raising awareness, there are walks, runs, cycling events; people climb mountains and skydive wearing the red End Polio Now t-shirt as a visible sign of support for the effort.

All of these activities keep the momentum going, provide critical funding, and share with the public the importance of polio eradication and the need to finish the job.

GPEI Q3. What message do you have for Rotary members and the general public about what they can do to help us make progress and reach our goal of a polio-free world as soon as possible?

We are going to get this done. So far this year, there are fewer cases than last year. We are making progress and must keep up the momentum. We made a promise to the children of the world. I encourage every Rotary member and every person who cares about children to help raise funds, donate, raise awareness about the benefits of vaccination, and encourage government support for polio eradication. Together, we end polio.

items.creator: Oxfam
items.title: Situation report on Bangladesh Flash Flood, July 2026
items.author: Oxfam
items.content:
Country: Bangladesh
Source: Oxfam

Please refer to the attached file.

Since 4 July 2026, continuous heavy rainfall associated with an active monsoon and a persistent low-pressure system over the north-west Bay of Bengal, combined with upstream runoff, has triggered widespread flash floods, severe waterlogging, and landslides across southeastern, eastern, and northeastern Bangladesh.

The disaster has affected more than 1.11 million people across 10 districts: Chattogram, Cox's Bazar, Sunamganj, Khagrachhari, Rangamati, Bandarban, Cumilla, Moulvibazar, Sylhet and Feni that resulting in 53 fatalities and 39 injuries as of today.

While Chattogram District has experienced the highest level of impact in terms of the affected population. Exceptional rainfall has been recorded across the affected areas. Chattogram city received 412.3 mm of rainfall within 24 hours till date, marking the highest single-day July rainfall recorded in the past 42 years.

The major rivers, including the Matamuhuri, Sangu, Surma, and Kushiyara, exceeded their danger levels, leading to embankment breaches, extensive flooding, and prolonged waterlogging.

Several lowlying areas were completely inundated, including the entirety of Banshkhali Upazila in Chattogram District. In the hill districts of Chattogram Division, saturated slopes triggered multiple landslides, causing casualties, damaging homes and infrastructure, and disrupting road connectivity.

Bangladesh’s flood threat is shifting from the country’s southeastern districts to the north and northeast, with forecasters warning that continued heavy rainfall and upstream runoff could trigger fresh flooding in Sylhet,
Sunamganj and Rangpur over the coming days.

The Bangladesh Meteorological Department (BMD) has forecast moderate to very heavy rainfall across much of the country over the next several days, raising concerns that the existing flood situation could worsen before improving

items.creator: Oxfam
items.title: Bangladesh: Floods Devastate Communities as Oxfam Scales Up Response
items.author: Oxfam
items.content:
Country: Bangladesh
Source: Oxfam

Please refer to the attached file.

DHAKA, 14 July 2026: Over 1.1 million people across Bangladesh have been badly affected by floods due to waterlogging and landslides triggered by heavy monsoon rainfall and upstream runoff since 4 July 2026. Chattogram has borne the heaviest impacts on people’s lives and livelihoods, while Cox’s Bazar, including the Rohingya refugee camps, continues to face flooding, landslide risks and damage to essential services.

“This is one emergency unfolding across two distinct contexts,” said Anil Pant, Country Director of Oxfam in Bangladesh. “The floods and their cascading impacts have severely disrupted people’s lives and livelihoods. In Chattogram and other affected districts, families have lost homes, income and access to safe drinking water and sanitation. In Cox’s Bazar, the danger is especially acute, as Rohingya families live in densely populated camps on fragile slopes, with limited space for safe relocation and heightened exposure to flooding and landslides.”

The floods have claimed dozens of lives, displaced thousands of people and damaged homes, roads, embankments, water points, latrines, cropland, fisheries and businesses. Many families have lost food stocks, bedding, cooking utensils and household assets. Thousands remain isolated in submerged communities, while some households cannot cook because they have neither dry space nor fuel.

As of today, the disaster has affected over 1.1 million people across 10 districts: Chattogram, Cox's Bazar, Rangamati, Khagrachhari, Bandarban, Sylhet, Sunamganj, Moulvibazar, and Habiganj, resulting in 53 fatalities and 39 injuries as of 14th July 2026. Safe drinking water, emergency food, shelter materials, hygiene and dignity kits, sanitation support and flexible cash assistance are among the most urgent needs. At least 3,500 water points and 12,400 latrines the damage to at least 3,500 water points and 12,400 latrines have heightened, heightening the threat of waterborne disease. Recovery will also require repairs to homes and WASH facilities and support to restore livelihoods.

“The water rose so quickly that we could save almost nothing. Our food, bedding and cooking utensils are gone, and finding clean water has become very difficult. We need food, safe drinking water and support to repair our home so that our family can start again,” said Akkas Ali, a flood-affected resident of Statkania, Chattogram.

In the Rohingya camps in Cox’s Bazar, 164 landslides and 42 flooding incidents have been reported. Fifteen Rohingya people lost their lives after shelters and facilities collapsed. A total of 9,707 people were displaced and temporarily relocated, while 482 weather-related incidents affected about 43,000 people across 9,463 households. Floodwater entered shelters and inundated WASH facilities, while prolonged cloudy weather left many households without lighting.

Women and girls, children, older people, individuals with disabilities, pregnant and breastfeeding women, and single-headed households face heightened protection and health risks. Limited relocation space, unstable slopes, damaged drainage and overcrowding are making the situation particularly dangerous and the vulnerabilities of the people living there reach the peak.

Oxfam in Bangladesh has initiated its emergency response and allocated BDT 12,065,000 (approximately BDT 12.07 million) to support the affected communities in Chattogram. Working through local partners, Oxfam is conducting rapid needs and gender assessments and coordinating with government authorities, the Needs Assessment Working Group, and UN clusters.

In Chattogram, Oxfam’s planned assistance includes emergency food, safe drinking water, hygiene & dignity kits containing soap, detergent, sanitary pads and oral rehydration salts, sari and lungi. and Multipurpose cash grants of BDT 8,000 per household are also planned so that vulnerable families can meet their urgent needs, including food, shelter repairs, healthcare, transport and livelihood inputs.

In Cox’s Bazar, Oxfam and partners are supporting assessments and repairing damaged facilities within operational areas. Oxfam has also provided personal protective equipment to 100 Rohingya volunteers serving as frontline responders.

“We are already on the ground with our partners, listening to affected communities and acting on their most urgent priorities,” Anil Pant said. “Our initial allocation is an important first step, but the scale of need is far greater. We call on donors, businesses, development partners and concerned citizens to provide flexible funding so that more families can receive life-saving assistance and rebuild with dignity.”

Oxfam aims to reach up to 160,000 people through a locally led, gender-responsive response focused on immediate relief, livelihood recovery and climate-resilient reconstruction with a funding aspiration of 3 million Euro.

For further information, please contact Md Sariful Islam, Head of Influencing, Communications, Advocacy and Media, Oxfam in Bangladesh, at +8801915631608/mdsaislam@oxfam.org.uk

items.creator: UN High Commissioner for Refugees
items.title: Jordan Operational Update - April-June 2026
items.author: UN High Commissioner for Refugees
items.content:
Countries: Jordan, Syrian Arab Republic
Source: UN High Commissioner for Refugees

Please refer to the attached file.

OPERATIONAL CONTEXT

In April, the Ministry of Planning and International Cooperation of Jordan initiated the planning phase of its 2026–2028 Strategic Framework for the Refugee Response. With UNHCR’s close engagement in the process, the framework aims to deliver an integrated and results-oriented approach in a context of constrained funding, while addressing refugee returns as a key strategic priority. In June, UNHCR released findings from the 2026 Socio-Economic Survey on Refugees in Jordan, conducted in collaboration with the World Bank and WFP. The survey, carried out every two years, covered a representative sample of 4,905 refugee households across camps and host communities, representing 24,752 registered refugees. Findings indicate persistently high levels of economic vulnerability, with an estimated 66 per cent of refugees living below the poverty line of 89 JOD per person per month.

items.creator: International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies
items.title: From Policy to Action: IFRC Supports Eswatini's New Disaster Risk Management Authority Through Anticipatory Action Initiatives
items.author: International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies
items.content:
Country: Eswatini
Source: International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies

Please refer to the attached file.

The Government of Eswatini has officially launched the National Disaster Risk Management Authority (NDRMA) on 28 April 2026 marking the transition from the former National Disaster Management Agency (NDMA) and a major shift in the country’s approach to disasters. This move signals a deliberate departure from a predominantly response‑oriented system towards a forward‑looking, risk‑informed and anticipatory framework. It reflects growing recognition that disasters are not isolated emergencies, but predictable and manageable risks that require sustained preparedness, early action, and long‑term resilience building.

The NDRMA is established under the National Disaster Risk Management Act of 2025, which provides a strengthened legal and institutional basis for disaster risk governance in Eswatini. While the NDMA played a vital role in coordinating disaster response and recovery, escalating climate‑related hazards, socio‑economic vulnerabilities, and recurrent shocks highlighted the need for a more comprehensive mandate.

The revised Act expands the authority’s responsibilities beyond emergency response to include disaster risk reduction, preparedness, anticipatory action, and resilience building. Launched by the Deputy Prime Minister Thulisile Dladla, the NDRMA represents a significant reform aimed at ensuring that disaster risk is addressed proactively and systematically, in support of sustainable development and the protection of vulnerable communities.

This step positions Eswatini among a growing number of countries in Southern Africa demonstrating leadership in proactive disaster risk management.

A Risk Context That Demands Anticipation

Eswatini’s disaster risk profile underscores the urgency and importance of this transition. The country is exposed to a range of hydrometeorological hazards, including recurrent droughts, flooding, severe storms and strong winds. These hazards intersect with structural vulnerabilities, including reliance on rain‑fed agriculture, pockets of poverty, and exposure of informal settlements. As a result, disasters in Eswatini often have disproportionate impacts on women, children, rural communities, and people depending on subsistence livelihoods.

Historically, responses to these shocks have been largely reactive, mobilizing resources after impacts have already occurred. While lifesaving, such approaches are costly and insufficient in the face of increasingly predictable climate hazards. The launch of the NDRMA represents a strategic shift to act earlier, smarter, and based on risk and the Deputy Prime Minister Dladla emphasized at the launch that “The authority must strengthen early warning systems so that timely and reliable information reaches all communities, including rural and vulnerable populations. It must ensure that disaster risk reduction is integrated into development planning, so that public investments are protected and future risks are reduced”.

From Management to Risk Governance

The evolution from the NDMA to the NDRMA reflects a deeper transformation in how disaster risk is understood and governed. Under the previous framework, disaster management was primarily centered on coordination of response, often triggered once impacts were already visible, but the National Disaster Risk Management Act broadens this scope by elevating disaster risk reduction and preparedness as core national priorities, embedding anticipatory action, enabling early responses based on forecasts, triggers, and risk analysis, promoting multi‑sectoral coordination, linking climate, water, agriculture, health, social protection, and infrastructure planning and supporting resilience building at community, institutional, and systems levels.

By institutionalizing these functions within the NDRMA, Eswatini is creating the legal and operational space to invest before crises escalate, reducing humanitarian needs and safeguarding development gains.

Creating an Enabling Environment for Anticipatory Action

The launch of the NDRMA is particularly significant in the context of anticipatory action, This new authority provides a stronger foundation for linking early warning systems with early action protocols, scaling forecast‑based financing and pre‑agreed response mechanisms, strengthening collaboration between national institutions, traditional structures, civil society, and international partners and bridging humanitarian action, disaster risk reduction, and climate adaptation

In a country where droughts and floods are increasingly predictable, anticipatory action has the potential to protect lives, livelihoods, and public resources, while reinforcing national ownership of disaster risk decisions.

Translating Policy into Early Action

The IFRC commends Eswatini for this forward‑looking reform and looks forward to seeing how the NDRMA advances anticipatory action, reduces disaster losses, and strengthens resilience for all EmaSwati.

The establishment of the NDRMA creates significant opportunities for Baphalali Eswatini Red Cross Society, supported by the International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies (IFRC), to further advance anticipatory action in Eswatini. With an active Drought Early Action Protocol, a Flood simplified Early Action Protocol under development, its strong community presence, volunteer network, and experience in early action, livelihoods support, and disaster preparedness, Baphalali is well positioned to support the operationalization of anticipatory action at local level linking national early warning systems to community‑based early action, targeting the most vulnerable households before shocks escalate. Through IFRC’s global expertise in forecast‑based financing, early action protocols, and risk‑informed programming, the Red Cross (Baphalali Eswatini Red Cross Society, IFRC, British Red Cross Society and Red Cross Red Crescent Climate Centre are already supporting the NDRMA in scaling anticipatory action mechanisms, strengthening risk analysis, developing pre‑agreed triggers, and promoting coordination across sectors. This partnership offers a pathway to translate policy into practice, ensuring that anticipatory action delivers timely, people‑centered and locally led protection of lives and livelihoods in the face of recurrent climate hazards.

A Commendable Step Forward

The launch of the National Disaster Risk Management Authority is more than an institutional rebranding, it is a signal of intent, the Deputy Prime Minister Dladla explained that “This transition is not only symbolic. It represents a significant change in the way Government intends to manage disaster risk. The country is moving from a system that was largely associated with disaster response to a stronger and more proactive system focused on risk reduction, preparedness, early warning, response, and recovery”. It reflects political commitment to protecting people not only when disasters occur, but before they unfold.

As climate risks intensify across Southern Africa, Eswatini’s proactive stance offers an encouraging example of how countries can move beyond response, embrace anticipation, and invest in resilience as a development priority. Continued success will depend on sustained resourcing, strong coordination, community engagement, and the effective operationalization of anticipatory and preparedness mechanisms but the foundation is now firmly in place.

Contact: Tawonga.dalikeni[@]ifrc.org

Under the previous framework, disaster management was primarily centered on coordination of response, often triggered once impacts were already visible, but the National Disaster Risk Management Act broadens this scope by elevating disaster risk reduction and preparedness as core national priorities, embedding anticipatory action, enabling early responses based on forecasts, triggers, and risk analysis, promoting multi‑sectoral coordination, linking climate, water, agriculture, health, social protection, and infrastructure planning and supporting resilience building at community, institutional, and systems levels.
By institutionalizing these functions within the NDRMA, Eswatini is creating the legal and operational space to invest before crises escalate, reducing humanitarian needs and safeguarding development gains.
Creating an Enabling Environment for Anticipatory Action
The launch of the NDRMA is particularly significant in the context of anticipatory action, This new authority provides a stronger foundation for linking early warning systems with early action protocols, scaling forecast‑based financing and pre‑agreed response mechanisms, strengthening collaboration between national institutions, traditional structures, civil society, and international partners and bridging humanitarian action, disaster risk reduction, and climate adaptation
In a country where droughts and floods are increasingly predictable, anticipatory action has the potential to protect lives, livelihoods, and public resources, while reinforcing national ownership of disaster risk decisions.
Translating Policy into Early Action
The IFRC commends Eswatini for this forward‑looking reform and looks forward to seeing how the NDRMA advances anticipatory action, reduces disaster losses, and strengthens resilience for all EmaSwati.
The establishment of the NDRMA creates significant opportunities for Baphalali Eswatini Red Cross Society, supported by the International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies (IFRC), to further advance anticipatory action in Eswatini. With an active Drought Early Action Protocol, a Flood simplified Early Action Protocol under development, its strong community presence, volunteer network, and experience in early action, livelihoods support, and disaster preparedness, Baphalali is well positioned to support the operationalization of anticipatory action at local level linking national early warning systems to community‑based early action, targeting the most vulnerable households before shocks escalate. Through IFRC’s global expertise in forecast‑based financing, early action protocols, and risk‑informed programming, the Red Cross (Baphalali Eswatini Red Cross Society, IFRC, British Red Cross Society and Red Cross Red Crescent Climate Centre are already supporting the NDRMA in scaling anticipatory action mechanisms, strengthening risk analysis, developing pre‑agreed triggers, and promoting coordination across sectors. This partnership offers a pathway to translate policy into practice, ensuring that anticipatory action delivers timely, people‑centered and locally led protection of lives and livelihoods in the face of recurrent climate hazards.
A Commendable Step Forward
The launch of the National Disaster Risk Management Authority is more than an institutional rebranding, it is a signal of intent, the Deputy Prime Minister Dladla explained that “This transition is not only symbolic. It represents a significant change in the way Government intends to manage disaster risk. The country is moving from a system that was largely associated with disaster response to a stronger and more proactive system focused on risk reduction, preparedness, early warning, response, and recovery”. It reflects political commitment to protecting people not only when disasters occur, but before they unfold.
As climate risks intensify across Southern Africa, Eswatini’s proactive stance offers an encouraging example of how countries can move beyond response, embrace anticipation, and invest in resilience as a development priority. Continued success will depend on sustained resourcing, strong coordination, community engagement, and the effective operationalization of anticipatory and preparedness mechanisms but the foundation is now firmly in place.

items.creator: Caritas
items.title: Nepal: A child rescued, a future restored: how cooperation can stop human trafficking
items.author: Caritas
items.content:
Countries: Nepal, India
Source: Caritas

When 14-year-old Sujan Kisku disappeared from his village in eastern Nepal, his family feared the worst.

Living in Jhapa District, Sujan’s parents depend on daily wage labour to support their family. When their son failed to return home on 25 June 2025, they searched desperately for days before seeking help from Caritas Nepal.

What Sujan later described was every parent’s nightmare.

According to his account, he had been abducted by two unidentified men on a motorcycle, threatened with knives and taken across the Nepal–India border. He recalls travelling for two days by train with little food while overhearing conversations suggesting that he was going to be sold.

Terrified but determined to survive, Sujan seized an opportunity when his abductors fell asleep during a train stop. Escaping unnoticed, he caught the attention of a passer-by who alerted the police. After receiving immediate protection, he was transferred to Don Bosco Abu Illam Child Welfare Centre in Coimbatore, southern India, where he remained in safe care.

As soon as Caritas Nepal was informed of the case, staff worked closely with local authorities, Nepal’s Embassy in New Delhi, child protection services and the welfare centre to coordinate the complex cross-border process needed to reunite Sujan with his family.

Following the necessary legal procedures, Sujan’s parents travelled to India to bring their son home.

Their reunion was filled with relief, tears and hope.

Today, Sujan is back with his family. He continues to receive psychosocial support to help him recover from the trauma, has returned to school and has been provided with a bicycle and school supplies to help him rebuild his future.

His story is a powerful reminder that behind every trafficking statistic is a child, a family and a community whose lives can be transformed through timely intervention, collaboration and compassion.

Human trafficking remains one of the world’s fastest-growing organised crimes, affecting millions of women, men and children. Combating it requires more than individual action. It demands cooperation across borders, strong local partnerships and a shared commitment to protecting human dignity.

This is the vision that brings together COATnet (Christian Organisations Against Trafficking Network), a global Christian network of more than 30 member organisations, including Caritas Internationalis, working together to prevent trafficking, protect survivors, promote safe migration and advocate for systemic change.

To strengthen collaboration across the network and make its knowledge, resources and members more accessible, COATnet is launching its new global website.

The new platform showcases the network’s mission, member organisations, country presence, resources and stories from around the world, helping connect those working every day to ensure that more children like Sujan can find safety, justice and hope.

Discover the new COATnet website: https://coatnet.global/

items.creator: Caritas
items.title: Nepal: Un niño rescatado, un futuro recuperado: cómo la cooperación puede poner fin a la trata de personas
items.author: Caritas
items.content:
Countries: Nepal, India
Source: Caritas

Cuando Sujan Kisku, de 14 años, desapareció de su aldea en el este de Nepal, su familia temió lo peor.

Los padres de Sujan, que viven en el distrito de Jhapa, dependen de trabajos a jornada para mantener a su familia. Cuando su hijo no regresó a casa el pasado 25 de junio de 2025, lo buscaron desesperadamente durante días antes de pedir ayuda a Caritas Nepal.

Lo que Sujan describió más tarde fue la pesadilla a la que todo padre teme.

Según su relato, dos hombres no identificados que iban en moto lo habían secuestrado, lo habían amenazado con cuchillos y lo habían llevado al otro lado de la frontera entre Nepal y la India. Recuerda haber viajado durante dos días en tren con poca comida, mientras escuchaba conversaciones que le hacían pensar que lo iban a vender.

Aterrorizado, pero decidido a sobrevivir, Sujan aprovechó la oportunidad cuando sus secuestradores se quedaron dormidos durante una parada de tren. Aunque logró escapar sin que nadie se diera cuenta, llamó la atención de un transeúnte que avisó a la policía. Tras recibir protección inmediata, fue trasladado al Centro de Bienestar Infantil Don Bosco Abu Illam en Coimbatore, al sur de la India, donde permaneció bajo cuidado seguro.

Tan pronto como Caritas Nepal tuvo conocimiento del caso, su personal colaboró en estrechamente con las autoridades locales, la embajada de Nepal en Nueva Delhi, los servicios de protección infantil y el centro de asistencia social para coordinar el complejo proceso transfronterizo necesario para reunir a Sujan con su familia.

Tras completar los trámites legales necesarios, los padres de Sujan viajaron a la India para traer a su hijo de vuelta a casa.

Su reencuentro estuvo lleno de alivio, lágrimas y esperanza.

Hoy, Sujan está de vuelta con su familia. Sigue recibiendo apoyo psicosocial para ayudarle a recuperarse del trauma, ha vuelto al colegio y se le ha proporcionado una bicicleta y material escolar para ayudarle a reconstruir su futuro.

Su historia es un poderoso recordatorio de que detrás de cada estadística sobre la trata hay un niño, una familia y una comunidad cuyas vidas pueden transformarse gracias a la intervención oportuna, la colaboración y la compasión.

La trata de personas sigue siendo uno de los delitos organizados que más rápido crece en el mundo y que afecta a millones de mujeres, hombres y niños. Para combatirla se necesita algo más que la acción individual. Exige cooperación transfronteriza, alianzas locales sólidas y un compromiso compartido con la protección de dignidad humana.

Esta es la visión que une a COATnet (Red de Organizaciones Cristianas contra la Trata de Personas ), una red cristiana mundial formada por más de 30 organizaciones miembros, incluida Caritas Internationalis, que trabajan juntas para prevenir la trata, proteger a los supervivientes, promover la migración segura y abogar por un cambio sistémico.

Con el fin de reforzar la colaboración en toda la red y facilitar el acceso a sus conocimientos, recursos y miembros, COATnet presenta su nueva página web global.

La nueva plataforma da a conocer la misión de la red, las organizaciones miembros, su presencia en los distintos países, los recursos y las historias de todo el mundo, lo que contribuye a conectar a quienes trabajan cada día para garantizar que más niños como Sujan puedan encontrar seguridad, justicia y esperanza.

Descubra el nuevo sitio web de COATnet: https://coatnet.global

The post Un niño rescatado, un futuro recuperado: cómo la cooperación puede poner fin a la trata de personas appeared first on Caritas.

items.creator: Caritas
items.title: Nepal: Un enfant sauvé, un avenir reconstruit : comment la coopération peut mettre fin à la traite des êtres humains
items.author: Caritas
items.content:
Countries: Nepal, India
Source: Caritas

Lorsque Sujan Kisku, 14 ans, a disparu de son village dans l’est du Népal, sa famille craignait le pire.

Installés dans le district de Jhapa, les parents de Sujan vivent de travaux journaliers pour subvenir aux besoins de leur famille. Lorsque leur fils n’est pas rentré chez lui le 25 juin 2025, ils l’ont cherché désespérément pendant des jours avant de demander de l’aide à Caritas Népal.

Ce que Sujan a raconté par la suite est le cauchemar de tout parent.

Selon son récit, il aurait été enlevé par deux hommes non identifiés circulant à moto, menacé à l’aide de couteaux et emmené de l’autre côté de la frontière entre le Népal et l’Inde. Il se souvient avoir voyagé pendant deux jours en train avec peu de nourriture tout en surprenant des conversations laissant entendre qu’il allait être vendu.

Terrifié mais déterminé à survivre, Sujan a profité de l’occasion qui s’est présentée lorsque ses ravisseurs se sont endormis pendant un arrêt du train. Il est parvenu à s’échapper sans être repéré et a attiré l’attention d’un passant qui a alerté la police. Après avoir été immédiatement mis à l’abri, il a été transféré au centre de protection de l’enfance Don Bosco Abu Illam à Coimbatore, dans le sud de l’Inde, où il a été pris en charge en toute sécurité.

Dès que Caritas Népal a été informée de la situation, ses équipes ont collaboré étroitement avec les autorités locales, l’ambassade du Népal à New Delhi, les services de protection de l’enfance et le centre d’accueil afin de coordonner les démarches transfrontalières complexes nécessaires au retour de Sujan auprès de sa famille.

Une fois les procédures juridiques nécessaires accomplies, les parents de Sujan se sont rendus en Inde pour ramener leur fils chez eux.

Les retrouvailles ont été empreintes de soulagement, de larmes et d’espoir.

Aujourd’hui, Sujan est de retour auprès de sa famille. Il continue de bénéficier d’un accompagnement psychosocial pour l’aider à surmonter son traumatisme. Il a repris le chemin de l’école et a également reçu un vélo et des fournitures scolaires pour l’aider à se reconstruire et à envisager l’avenir.

Son histoire nous rappelle avec force que derrière chaque statistique sur la traite des êtres humains se trouvent un enfant, une famille et une communauté dont la vie peut être transformée grâce à une intervention rapide, à la coopération et à la solidarité.

La traite des êtres humains reste l’une des formes de criminalité organisée qui connaissent la croissance la plus rapide dans le monde et touche des millions de femmes, d’hommes et d’enfants. Pour la combattre, les actions individuelles ne suffisent pas. Il faut une coopération transfrontalière, des partenariats locaux solides et un engagement commun à protéger la dignité humaine.

C’est autour de cette vision que s’articule l’action de COATnet (Christian Organisations Against Trafficking Network), un réseau chrétien mondial regroupant plus de 30 organisations membres, dont Caritas Internationalis, qui œuvrent ensemble à la prévention de la traite, à la protection des personnes qui en ont été victimes, à la promotion de migrations sûres et à la mise en place de changements systémiques.

Pour renforcer la collaboration au sein du réseau et de rendre ses connaissances, ses ressources et ses membres plus accessibles, COATnet lance son nouveau site internet mondial https://coatnet.global/

La nouvelle plateforme présente la mission du réseau, ses organisations membres, son implantation dans différents pays, ses ressources et ldes témoignages venus du monde entier. Elle contribue ainsi à mettre en relation les personnes et les organisations qui œuvrent chaque jour pour que davantage d’enfants comme Sujan puissent retrouver sécurité, justice et espoir.

Découvrez le nouveau site de COATnet : https://coatnet.global

items.creator: World Health Organization
items.title: WHO Weekly Situation Update on Ongoing Health Emergency Events in South Sudan - Week #: 28; Date: 06 - 12 Jul 2026
items.author: World Health Organization
items.content:
Countries: South Sudan, Democratic Republic of the Congo
Source: World Health Organization

Please refer to the attached file.

EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

South Sudan remains in EVD/BVD preparedness and readiness mode with no confirmed cases to date, despite sustained regional risk; DR Congo has now reported 1,926 confirmed cases and 702 deaths (CFR 36.5%). Cumulative PoE screening rose sharply from 394,410 to 552,071 travellers (157,661 additional), and the National Master Training of Trainers was completed, producing a pool of 90 trainers. Two EVD alerts (Yambio and Tambura) were investigated and discarded. Cholera declined 23.7% week-on-week (347 new cases), with the OCV mop-up campaign set for 10 July. Polio detected a new cVDPV1 environmental isolate in Juba; measles and rubella reported no new cases this week, and Guinea Worm remains at zero cases for 2026.

items.title: Afghanistan: WHO EMR Polio Bulletin Issue No. 1449, Week 27 (ending on 12/07/2026)
items.author: World Health Organization
items.content:
Countries: Afghanistan, Djibouti, occupied Palestinian territory, Pakistan, Somalia, Sudan, Yemen
Source: World Health Organization

Please refer to the attached file.

Wild poliovirus type 1 (WPV1)

Cases: Four new WPV1 positive cases in 2026 from Afghanistan reported this week. The new WPV1 cases are: 2 from Nangarhar province (Behsud and Rodat districts), 1 each from Hilmand province (Musaqalah district) and Hirat province (Shindand district) noting that the Hirat/Shindand case was identified by positive contacts. In total, 52 WPV1-positive cases have been confirmed in 2025 (21 from Afghanistan and 31 from Pakistan in 2025) and 14 WPV1 cases reported in 2026 (11 from Afghanistan and 3 from Pakistan).

Environment: Thirteen new WPV1 positive environmental samples in 2026 were reported this week, 12 from Afghanistan and 1 from Pakistan. The new WPV1 environmental samples of Afghanistan are: 5 from Kandahar province (all from Kandahar district), 4 from Nangarhar province (2 from Behsud, and 1 each from Batikot and Jalalabad districts), 2 from Kabul province (both from Kabul district), and 1 from Hilmand province (Lashkargah district), with dates of collection on 20th and 24th May 2026, while the new WP1 environmental sample from Pakistan was reported from Khyber Pakhtunkhwa province (Peshawar district) with collection date on 11th June 2026. items.creator: Karen Human Rights Group
items.title: Myanmar: Dooplaya District Incident Report: Burma Army soldiers arrested and beat four villagers, including a 16-year-old, in Noh T’Kaw Township (March 2026)
items.author: Karen Human Rights Group
items.content:
Country: Myanmar
Source: Karen Human Rights Group

Please refer to the attached file.

Excerpt

Part 3 – Complete Description of the Incident

Describe the Incident(s) in complete detail.

Seik Gyi [Kyainseikgyi] Town in Dooplaya District is under the control of the SAC [State Administration Council]. Villagers from both nearby and distant areas were travelling through Seik Gyi Town. Some villagers from distant areas did not know the alternative routes to avoid the town, so they passed through it. Police in Seik Gyi Town, along with SAC soldiers and immigration officers, were operating and conducting checks at the entrance of the town on the main road. This checkpoint is located in the Seik Gyi Town–Kyon Kone area, where multiple groups [of the Burma Army] are involved in the checking.

On 20 March 2026, at around 10 am, Saw A---, Saw B---, Saw C---, and Saw D--- accompanied two friends, both women, from Aa--- village, Lay Wah Hka village tract, Noh T’Kaw (Kyainseikgyi) Township, Dooplaya District, who were travelling to another village via Seik Gyi Town. When they arrived at the checkpoint, the SAC stopped them but did not stop the two women. The SAC asked questions in Burmese, but the young people only spoke Karen, so they did not understand what they were being asked. When they [the Burma Army] checked Saw A---’s phone, they saw a picture of his friend wearing camouflage trousers. Then, the SAC [soldiers] punched each of them [the four men] in the face at least three times. After that, they were handcuffed and forced to kneel with their hands raised. They were then hit with a stick at least 30 times each.

As the two women were not stopped by the SAC at the checkpoint, they went to Ab--- village, Noh T’Kaw village tract, Noh T’Kaw Township. They informed one of their friends in Ab--- village about the incident. Then, their friend asked for help from a BGF [Border Guard Force][7] checkpoint authority in the village. The BGF checkpoint authority contacted the SAC and informed them that those young people were civilians, not enemies [members of an armed group]. Therefore, on 20 March 2026, at about 11 am, the SAC released them. After they were released, they returned to the village and did not continue their journey. [After release, they did not seek formal treatment at a medical facility.]

In March 2026, [a KHRG researcher conducted an interview with Saw A--- and] he explained that after he returned home, he could not move his body easily or sleep well at night because of his injury. He even needed to take medicine to reduce the pain. Saw A--- added: “I could not breathe well and felt dizzy when they hit me. I could not bear the pain, and I thought it would be better if they shot me dead. Last night, I could not sleep well. I cannot lie on my back for long, so I sometimes have to sleep lying prone.”

Saw A---'s injury had worsened, and he was experiencing more pain when the KHRG researcher asked him about his injury again [later] in March 2026.

items.title: Cambodia: Agrometeorological 10 days Bulletin for 11–20 July 2026 Across the Northern Tonle Sap Basin of the PEARL Project
items.author: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations
items.content:
Country: Cambodia
Source: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations

Please refer to the attached file.

Flood Early Warning

With floods forecasted during the next 10 days, protect roots from standing water and secure the orchard. On low ground, raise soil mounds around young trees 30-50 cm (about knee height) but do not bury the graft union. Open diversion channels one hand wide and one palm deep to lead water to safe outlets; keep inlets free of trash. Move tools, fertilizers, and crates to higher shelves (about one palm above floor). Mulch 10-15 cm (about a palm to a palm-and-a- half) with dry rice straw or chopped prunings, keeping a hand-width gap at the trunk. Harvestable fruit in very low plots should be picked early and stored above floor level. After floods recede, remove (rogue) trees that do not recover and dispose of plant waste away from the orchard; wash tools with soapy water.

items.creator: Agency for Technical Cooperation and Development
items.title: Environmental & Social Impact Assessment: Assessment of STREAM Programme Water Infrastructure in Small Watersheds of Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, and Uzbekistan (April - May 2025)
items.author: Agency for Technical Cooperation and Development
items.content:
Countries: Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, Uzbekistan
Sources: Agency for Technical Cooperation and Development, IMPACT Initiatives

Please refer to the attached file.

SUMMARY

The Syr Darya River Basin, spanning Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, and Uzbekistan, is a crucial water source for many communities in the Fergana Valley. Despite its importance, the basin lacks centralised management, leading to frequent water distribution issues, particularly for downstream countries.1 Water originating in upstream countries often fails to reach downstream areas in sufficient quantities, exacerbating tension among communities along the border. The river is vital for agriculture, providing irrigation for crops, water for livestock, and supporting hydropower generation. Consequently, effective management and equitable distribution of the Syr Darya's resources are essential for the region’s stability and prosperity.

To address climate change challenges affecting natural resource management in the watersheds of the Fergana Valley, Acted, IMPACT, and International Alert, with support from European Commission and the PATRIP Foundation, launched the STREAM programme in 2022.3 The findings from the STREAM programme’s inception phase highlighted the interconnectedness of climate change, industrial activities, and natural resource management.4 A recent comprehensive assessment of the Isfana Watershed, funded by EuropeAid and implemented with partner Central Asian Alliance for Water (CAAWE), revealed systemic water loss in the irrigation network due to outdated and deteriorating infrastructure and lack of data on waterflow.

To tackle these challenges with systemic water loss, Acted plans to utilise PATRIP funds for the "hard component" of the programme, focusing on infrastructure rehabilitation and the installation of water measuring devices to improve water monitoring and reduce water loss. A technical feasibility study, conducted by the organisations WAPCOS and KIBERA, was further commissioned by Acted to identify the most suitable locations for water meters and canal rehabilitation, aiming to enhance water efficiency, and was completed in March 2025.

In order to ensure that the selected interventions meet the, “do no harm” requirements of both PATRIP’s Environmental and Social Policy requirements, and conform to Acted’s own intervention requirements, the following Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) was conducted in order to minimise or prevent negative environmental and social risks and impacts of the programme activities, including irrigation infrastructure rehabilitation and water meter installation, that may occur in the communities of intervention. By enhancing environmental and social benefits at the operational level, this assessment facilitates the identification and mitigation of risks and negative impacts, enabling continuous monitoring and improvement of the programme's environmental and social performance.

To do this, IMPACT adopted a workshop-based approach outlined in the Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation’s (SDC) Climate, Environment and Disaster Risk Reduction Integration Guidance (CEDRIG) toolkit. 7 This is a participatory methodology that aims to mainstream climate change, environmental sustainability, and disaster risk reduction considerations into development interventions, while also taking an inclusive approach that considers feedback from the community and ensuring Accountability to Affected Populations (AAP).

In each location, approximately 18-22 local stakeholders from local government, community leadership, and civil society, were invited for a 1-day workshop. This workshop utilised a series of guided modules, in which participants were asked to provide information in a structured format that could then be systematically analysed by the IMPACT team afterwards. The workshop was divided into 3 parts; 1) the participants were first introduced to the planned infrastructure, its locations, timeframes and outputs; 2) participants then assessed the likely hazards that may affect the successful implementation of the infrastructure, and 3) participants assesses the potential negative impacts that the infrastructure may have on the community. For both the hazards and negative impacts, participants then ranked both their likelihood and severity to determine their overall risk. Of the top selected risks, participants then provided potential locally-based mitigation measures that could be included in the planned construction which could reduce the likelihood or impact of these hazards and negative impacts, thereby ensuring more sustainable and community-lead project outcomes.

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Earthquakes in Venezuela: Situation Report #21 (14 July 2026, Time: 09:00 pm)
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of)
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached file.

Situation Report No. 21
Earthquakes in Venezuela
14 July 2026
Time: 9:00 pm

Highlights

  • Following the 24 June earthquakes of magnitude 7.2 and 7.5 (18:00 local time), authorities report more than 1,200 aftershocks.
  • Official toll: 4,734 people dead and 16,740 injured.
  • A total of 6,462 people have been rescued.
  • An additional appeal for US$298 million was launched to scale up the earthquake response and address the multisectoral needs of 1.3 million people over the next six months.
  • The United Nations system and its partner organizations, in coordination with the Government, are responding with multisectoral actions and will continue to scale up assistance to affected people.

Situation overview

Venezuela is facing a national emergency following two consecutive high-magnitude earthquakes (7.2 and 7.5) that struck on 24 June (18:00 local time) in the north-central region, with the epicentre located along the San Felipe–Yumare–Montalbán axis (Yaracuy/Carabobo). Authorities report 1,275 aftershocks.

Twenty days since the earthquakes, authorities report a toll of 4,734 people dead, 16,740 injured and 6,462 rescued since the onset of the emergency. Authorities also indicated that 17,907 people lost their home.

The Government response remains focused on search operations, the management of transitional camps and assistance to affected populations. The United Nations and its partner organizations, in coordination with the authorities, continue implementing multisectoral response activities and scaling up assistance. United Nations Under-Secretary-General for Humanitarian Affairs and Emergency Relief Coordinator Tom Fletcher announced a US$298 million addendum to the Humanitarian Response Plan aimed at addressing the multisectoral needs of 1.3 million people over the next six months. This is in addition to the 5.5 million people already targeted under the 2026 Humanitarian Response Plan, which requires US$632 million in funding.
